sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
I’m not sure which was worse – the pain in my foot or having to watch Rob, Ed, Chris x 2 and Pete fly over my house on the way to goal in Tadcaster on 15 August!  The results of my MRI scan finally came back revealing minor bone oedema between the 3rd and 4th metatarsal, probably due to arthritis aggravated by injury.  The 18 week waiting list for the NHS physio wasn’t appealing so, difficult as it is for a Yorkshireman, I forked out to go private.  He told me that I should be OK as long as I keep my foot supported, so it’s definitely boots for me in the future.  I’ve flown twice since the MRI confirmed that it wasn’t a stress/hairline fracture, without any dramas.

I’m pleased to be able to report some progress on finding some volunteers to serve you on next year’s committee.  Rob Urselmann has offered to take over from Clive Sury as Safety Officer; allowing Clive to take over as Club Secretary.  That would allow Tam to take over from me as Chairman.  It’s a back up plan: far from ideal because you’ll be losing a lot of continuity: me this year, Tam next year, and who knows who else?  Still plenty of capacity for new volunteers if you think you can help out.

sections.title: Basic Rotary
sections.content:
Learning to fly helicopters was split into 2 phases: learning to fly the helicopter itself, with civilian instructors; and then learning to fly a helicopter in a tactical environment, with military instructors.  Both phases were conducted in the Gazelle helicopter: a single engined ‘sports car’ of a flying machine with a bubble type of cockpit.  It was affectionately known as the ‘whistling chicken leg’ because of its shape and the noise that its distinctive Fenestron tail rotor made.  The undercarriage consisted of skids rather than wheels.

Gazelle AH 1 - ‘The whistling chicken leg’

Despite the Gazelle being a fairly simple helicopter, one of the most intimidating aspects for most of us was learning to start the bloody thing.  For a student, memorising the sequence of switches and checks was mind boggling.  It wasn’t unusual for people to construct a mocked-up cockpit at home in order to practise.  Biddy Baxter, the editor of Blue Peter, would have been envious of the cardboard boxes and gaffer tape, with instruments and switches crudely drawn onto them.  In early flights it wasn’t unusual for half the sortie to be taken up with starting and closing down the aircraft.  (I later progressed to 2 engines which took even longer!)

Gazelle Central console.  More levers and switches in the roof and between the pilots.

Flying a helicopter in forward flight is much like flying a fixed wing aircraft.  The challenge comes when trying to hover.  The general teaching technique is to start with one control at a time (with the instructor controlling the others) – pedals for yaw, collective for up/down (mercifully the ‘throttle’ in the Gazelle is controlled automatically), and cyclic for horizontal movement.  Once each control has been mastered individually, combinations of pairs of controls are practised, with the instructor taking control back each time the student becomes overloaded.  To add to the complexity each control input has a secondary effect which has to be anticipated to maintain a steady hover.  Most students over-control to start with.  The solution is to relax – easier said than done!

 Mastering the hover.

A slight amount of movement isn’t of great concern until it comes to landing where a bit of lateral movement can result in the aircraft rolling over and thrashing itself to death.  Any lateral movement during landing and the drill was to climb back into the hover, stabilise and try again.  The doctor on our course likened it to an elderly person trying to lower themselves onto the toilet!  (Years later I got to practice landing on a ship – the ‘earth’ certainly moved for me!)

Much time was spent at the ‘hover squares’: white lines painted onto the grass on a level field, about the size of half a football pitch.  Each square had a diagonal cross and a circle inside it.  The idea was to practice moving forwards, sideways and backwards, and yawing, all at a steady hover height.  Once you’d convinced the instructor that you were unlikely to crash he’d get out and let you practice by yourself.  By this stage of the course we were moving into the summer months and I remember dripping with sweat from the concentration.  Each instructor had 2 students and my buddy told me that whilst our instructor had been getting out he’d accidentally caught his foot under the collective and the aircraft shot 10 feet into the air with the instructor dangling out of the door!

One of our number had experience of flying model helicopters and we were sure that he’d pick up the necessary skills more quickly than the rest of us.  He was booted off the course at an early stage.

Slowly we progressed to solo flights and circuits, and from there to more advanced manoeuvres.  Perhaps the most difficult was landing in confined areas (clearings in woods).  This was an area where I struggled.  After one flight with my instructor I was given a ‘red’ (fail).  The normal procedure having received ‘a red’ was to be placed onto review for remedial training, but instead my instructor sent me out solo to practice landing in confined areas!?  I can only assume that he thought that I lacked confidence, and this was his way of building it up.  It worked: I didn’t kill myself and  progressed to Advanced Rotary Wing where the military instructors were less generous..      

 

The F’uglies.

sections.title: Another Step forwards…maybe
sections.content:
John Edmonds reports

Continuing from my CP and beyond adventures in Bulgaria I wanted to get to fly on home turf and start ticking off tasks for the Pilot badge. I can then join the rest of you on train rides and hitching lifts home after glorious 8 hour flights….the maybe!

I had a super day on Windbank even though it was a bit strong. The take off was, well, just that, I popped the wing up and was swept away to start soaring along the ridge. I was able to get over the valley but a tempting thermal had me turning until I was back over the ridge. OK, time to find out what the speed bar does on a Geo 3. I can report it has nothing to do with forward speed but greatly increases the rate of descent, at least it got me out of the venturi and I was able to get back over the valley for a bit more floating and then down towards the cars.

It has become apparent that there are huge gaps in my abilities, not so much the flying though I still have lots to go at there too, more the stuff on the ground. Overseas and the teaching there is about all forwards launch and in our more unpredictable and changeable weather the reverse launch is preferred.

YouTube and tutorials are great but there’s now’t like being out there and ‘doing it’. So that was my plan until a fall, broken arm, T’s 3,4 & 5 damaged and a couple of broken ribs with bruising, especially from ‘her in doors’....why were you up a ladder cutting trees in your shorts? There’s no answer really.

 

Anyway, on the road to recovery and rising spirits had me back out to get to grips with this bag of lines and cloth. I’m fortunate, I have easy access to some land which takes a north through east and south so have been out there. There are aches, some explained some not, so it’s steady as you go, ah the wonders of youth and health…..

I read the books and thought I understood, it’s the subtlety needed which I have to start to appreciate. You need to bear in mind that my muscle memory from flying hangliders is good for speed and height, am I going up or down, will I make the landing target, but when you lift up a hanglider, it wants to fly and it is hard work keeping it on the ground so generally it’s, glider on the ground lift the nose get it on your shoulders, balanced, and run. None of this cobra stuff, build a wall! What’s that all about?

So I am embarked on a self imposed safety campaign to master the dark art of Ground Handling. Every now and again I am able to get the glider above my head and keep it there, turn and move left a bit, right a bit but not with the consistency needed.

Things I am slowly learning:

1 Face the wing, I thought I was but it turns out that I was putting one hip forwards and that small, 4cm movement, would add tension to one side and steer the glider, send it off the wrong way.

2 Don’t hold all the A’s, the middle ones are enough. The books and my tutors say, have all the A’s in your hand, that’s great unless it’s a bit switchy. Middle A’s get the centre sorted and the rest will follow, that seems a lot easier.

3 Don’t jerk at the controls, smooth and anticipate so stop the input actually before the wing has gone to where you want it to, it will be there in a second or so. I was trying to fly the way I would my little stunt kite, well there the same arn’t they apart from the scale?

4 Once the ‘wall’ looks solid, use the body to pull the wing up with the A’s by rocking backwards, hips square, see above!

5 Slow the wing with the brakes before it just gets overhead, out of the power window, then release to let it fly. I was trying to control the assent so it went up at the rate I wanted rather than letting it fly.

6 This is a big one, how to kill the glider, get it down. Easy, pull on the C’s. Turn, where are the C’s, argh, I’m having to run and find lines other than the ones in my hand. Don’t worry, you can’t run that fast so it will stop when you fall over unless the wing has other ideas and is still inflated and wants to carry on down wind with you still attached!

This is the list so far….. Now you may see me or other, I will call us pilots though we don’t have the stripes yet, not struggling but not having a fun time with a misbehaving wing. This not having fun can soon change to bodily damage so don’t hang back. Please, you may not be a qualified coach but you have experience. If it looks bad, step in, I’m sure you would without me saying. If we are having trouble ask if help would be appreciated, ‘What are you trying to do?’ It’s a pleasure to see people chatting on the hill and at socials and that is what the flying community should be like.

I am looking forward to getting confident on the ground so I can really enjoy my next 6m/s climb, skimming the edge of the white stuff and know that the last few meters going down won’t hurt. See you there!!

News Flash….Latest….Flights booked to FlyPiedrahitra…there could be another report next month!

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content: Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports

subsections.title: Discretion and having a good model of the conditions.
subsections.content:
https://www.xcontest.org/world/en/flights/detail:vikingforties/10.8.2025/10:52 

I had the good fortune to get to the coast with a flight from Bradwell in August. I’d had a bit of a low point just coming up to Market Weighton but then the slope of the Wolds had kicked off and it was plain sailing to goal at Driffield. A few had then gone further to land at Bridlington and there’s more transport options so I decided to do the same and had thoughts of a beach landing, ice cream and a short walk to the station.

As the coast came into view on what looked like an easy glide, my spidey senses were telling me something was not right. At 3500 feet my ground speed was up to 70km heading east… the direction out to sea! However, I’m looking at two sets of wind turbines south of Brid that are showing a very definite and strong south easterly (i.e. I should be going into wind). What is going on here!?

A bit more context. We’d set the goal at Driffield because RASP had shown a very clear sea breeze that day marching inland and probably stopping us getting to Bridlington. It also showed strong wind at the coast anyway so not the ideal conditions for landing there.

I decided that now I was there, conditions were sort of as forecast but not quite. What I wanted to avoid was being at 3000ft with a back wind that I couldn’t fight and heading out to sea. I hadn’t brought an inflatable toy and that would definitely spoil my day. The interface between meteo wind and the sea breeze must be much lower than me. I wound off the height in a spiral well inland and did a few test reversals into wind to make sure I was not going out to sea anymore.

It was not until below 500m AMSL that I entered the sea breeze and it was a strong one. So I then had the voice of sitting on bar and creeping to the beach with a land out in a golf course or caravan park or making a safe landing in a field at the edge of town. Discretion was the better part of valour here.

What do you do when your spidey sense goes off and you feel that something is not quite right…

  • Make a model in your head - pickup cues and work out what you think is going on.
    My ground speed and the wind turbines were at odds and the sea breeze must be below me.

  • Decide how to test the model - what will confirm your idea of the conditions?
    I could descend until I contacted the sea breeze.

  • Decide on a course of action.
    Wind off the height in a spiral and potentially land early away from the hazard.

    Incidentally, I decided on a spiral because I thought 30kph of back wind contacting 20kph of seabreeze at an engle is going to lead to some turbulence. A spiral keeps your wind well pressured which will resist collapses. In the end I barely noticed descending through the interface.

What a day!

sections.title: Sites and News
sections.content:
Pete Logan reports

You’ve probably noticed that the Weather station on Yorburgh above Bainbridge has been a bit quiet since early July. We’ve got the box down and are diagnosing the fault which has been a bit tricky to work out. Holfuy, the manufacturers, are sending out a replacement mobile phone modem board to hopefully sort out the comms problem. With any luck it should be back up in a couple of weeks.

Helping to diagnose were these two blasts from the past….

And here’s your handy weather crew in action…



Keep an eye on this to see when it’s back…
https://holfuy.com/en/station-info/station=1390 .

sections.title: The Baildon Sod 2025
sections.content:
Pete Logan reports

Baildon Moor got its own version of the Birdman of Bognor competition last month as Dales folks gathered for the 48th edition of the Baildon Sod. They battled nil wind, sweaty conditions and chest deep bracken to launch on the shallow slope for an earnest glide competition.

The early lead was set by Baildon local, Pete Logan, with a clean launch and flight across the 17th fairway of the golf course below. This was soon overtaken by Andy and Sue Berzins, who'd come from the Wolds for this competition.

Eventual winner was current British Champion, Richard Butterworth, who turned up with his Enzo 3 and red Submarine Harness. It was too much for the rest of the twenty or so fliers as he set the standard flying to almost the end of the 18th fairway. Well done Richard!

After all the bracken thrashing and exertion, they retired to The Malt Shovel in Baildon centre for a "debrief".

Full photographs and videos at:

https://nx35281.your-storageshare.de/apps/photos/public/nBHE6LexJbEKMEQUWY1VqbBK0gTCYI2H

 

Dave comes out of retirement.


Winner Richard Butterworth and the first and only time someone is going to bother to turn up to Baildon in a sub!


Jordan became a “Darwen Award winner” after flying this fine ‘90s harness.

Groundsman Andy, preparing the turf.

A tight moment as the gods of wind decide whether it’s going to be a visit to the bracken.

A beautiful night for it….

sections.title: Social News
sections.content:
Stef Sykes reports

FARMERS AND FLYERS DINNER 2025

All welcome to this annual celebration of the gift of take-offs and landings.

On 18th October all members are invited to come to the Knights Table in Stainforth to eat well (3 courses of very good grub for £27), chat with our farming friends and flying colleagues alike.

This year we have a special offer of a FREE PLACE for each committee member alongside a 2 for 1 offer on the first 10 members to message the social sec following this announcement.

Our fine Chair dissolves the audience with his fine tales alongside a medley of films of members escapades…

Do come, it's fun.

Also…

DALES FLY IN 2025.

This is happening next weekend. If you've not yet got a ticket, there are some still available through Eventbrite. Just search dales flying 2025.

This will feature a new set of challenges set by Ed Cleasby. All details on the booking page.

Weather is looking tricky, but knowing our luck, it'll come good.

See you on the hill.

sections.title: Farmers’ Dinner
sections.content: The Knights Table

subsections.title: Saturday 18th October 2025
subsections.content:
7:30pm for 8pm dinner     

We are very lucky that we don’t pay any site fees in the Dales.  Instead we invite the farmers and their partners to a slap up meal every year. Over the years we have found this to be very cost effective: a little goodwill goes a very long way.  The committee does most of the hosting, and this year we will also be offering free tickets to the first club coaches who respond, by way of thanks for giving up their spare time.  Other members are most welcome, but I’m afraid that you’ll have to pay.  £27 for 3 courses isn’t bad these days, and getting to know the farmers can be very handy when you need a lift back up the hill…

As with the previous couple of years, we’re returning to the excellent food and service of Knights Table at Stainforth.  Please aim to meet in the bar for drinks from 7.30pm.  The dress code is Smart Casual, but please wear whatever makes you feel comfortable.

subsections.title: Booking
subsections.content:
If you would like to attend, please respond to Dave Bradwell, treasurer@dhpc.org.uk  by 8 October 2025.  He’ll need to know your full name (first & surname) and then the three numbers of your preferred dishes.  If you’re bringing a guest, then he’ll need the same for them.  If you need to pay he’ll tell you how to do that.

We look forward to seeing you there.

subsections.title: Menu - £27 for 3 courses
subsections.content:
Starters

1 Gyozas - crispy roasted pork dumplings deep fried served with hoi sin dipping sauce and pickled cucumbers

2 Samosas - spiced potato and spinach samosas with bowl of tarka dal and mango chutney - V, VE

3 Soup of the Day - a piping hot bowl of our homemade soup served with homemade bread and butter - GF

4 Popcorn Prawns - plump Greenland prawns tossed in salt and pepper coating deep fried  crispy and served with pea shoots and sweet chilli dipping sauce - GF

Main Courses

5 Steak and Onion Pie - slow cooked Maudsley farm beef with rich local ale gravy in an all-butter shortcrust pastry, served with vegetable selection and chips

6 Fish and Chips - fresh Fleetwood haddock fillet deep fried in a light ale batter served with  mushy peas and chips - GF

7 Grandma’s Cheese Pie - an old family recipe, three cheeses, onion and potato oven baked in a shortcrust  pastry pie served with salad and chips - V

8 Sheep and Pigs - slow roasted crispy belly pork, Maudsley farm lamb chop over smoked ham bubble n  squeak in a red wine and peppercorn gravy with a side of seasonal veg - GF

9 Goan Vegetable Curry - cauliflower, red pepper, green beans and potato cooked in our mild Goan spice paste with tomato, coconut and tamarind served with vegetable pilaf and bhel puri - GF, V, VE

Desserts

10 Blackberry & Apple Crumble Tart with vanilla ice cream

11 White Chocolate Cheesecake with blueberry compote - GF

12 Chocolate and Stem Ginger Meringues layered with whipped cream and chocolate sauce - GF

13 White Chocolate and Raspberry Tiramisu

Vegan desserts available on request

GF – Gluten Free Available V – Vegetarian VE – Vegan Available Please inform a member of staff if you have any allergies, intolerances or special dietary requirements. While we try our best to  accommodate allergies, we are not an allergen-free kitchen, so we cannot guarantee everything is 100% allergen free.

sections.title: Safety
sections.content:
Clive Sury reports

Paragliding – a solo or a group activity?

Originally, I was going to headline this article ‘I get by with a little help from my friends’ since I’ll be sharing examples of where other flyers have been very kind to me in proffering guidance over the last month. However, this must be muted given the experience of a Dales member from last Friday that I’ll also illuminate upon.

First focusing on the positive through the following recent experiences:

·         Ground handling on Windbank I lost control of my glider.  Joseph Edmonds came over, checked if I’d like feedback on my mistake and then coached me through how to control the wing in higher winds.

·         I went up Whernside and took longer to get up the hill than Jordan Whitaker and James Maxwell (no surprises there).  When setting up, Jordan flew past and shouted the Dales radio frequency they were using to check I could remain in contact with them in the air as a backup.

·         On Gragareth I’d just taken off when Tom Shuttleworth introduced himself on the radio to let me know that he was also on the hill about to fly.  Plus, crucially, to inform me that the wind was very strong with the Venturi effect and therefore, to avoid or be very mindful of this if considering any top landings.

In each instance I’m extremely grateful for the advice and assistance; helping to make me a better and/or safer flyer.  Therefore, thank you Joseph, Jordan and Tom for this and at the risk of this becoming like an Oscar’s acceptance speech, everyone else who’s been so supportive.  Paragliding has much to commend it as a hobby, not least because of the collegiate spirit of the community as demonstrated here.

Against this, I must share another flyer’s experience on Dodd Fell last Friday.  The pilot was on the top launch area and whilst attempting to take off was instead dragged along the hill and hit a rocky outcrop.  This caused a severe internal puncture and bashed leg.  At the time there was another flyer about 20m above him and, even though the injured pilot had landed in a depression, he initially expected this other flyer (who was not strapped in) to come over and enquire about his condition.  One cannot know for certain if this other flyer had seen the aborted take-off and dragging or notice that the glider had not become airborne and therefore, question why not.  However, this incident does beg the question of whether someone could have helped a severely injured pilot, but for whatever reason chose not to do so.  In any event, this article’s purpose is to raise awareness of pilots to keep others in sight and be prepared to offer support where required.  Of note is that Rob Urselmann is organising a First Aid Training Day in the near future to help with how someone could deal with these situations.

In the event, the injured pilot managed to get himself to hospital and is now in a stable condition.  It’s pretty obvious that it would have been much better if somebody else had helped pack his kit and taken him to hospital.

Therefore, in summary, I’d like to thank people for when they’ve shown consideration to others but also to raise awareness of the need to keep a look out for others and to be prepared to support as needed.

Incidents to report:

·         Dodd Fell – Friday 15th August – Pilot dragged on launch – as above

·         Wether Fell – Friday 15th August – Tandem collision with hill – injuries to the passenger who is now in a stable condition.

Both these accidents will have incident reports completed soon.

Safe flying,

Clive.

Martin Baxter comments:  For me there are 3 key lessons:

  • If you see a pilot go down, or get dragged, please take the time to check that they are OK.
  • If you have a mishap and need help then don’t be afraid to use your radio to call for help.
  • Make sure that your radio is on the Dales frequency: 143.850.

sections.title: First Aid Course 29 November
sections.content:
Rob Urselmann reports

Above incidents are another good reminder that you might be called upon at any, and always unexpected, moment to provide first aid. Do you know / remember how to stem a severe bleed? Do you carry even a basic first aid pack?

In case you haven’t heard yet or have been dragging your feet, now is your opportunity to sign up for the incredibly good value DHPC one-day, paragliding-focussed first aid course

  • 29 November  09:00 - 17:00
  • Again at the Bradley Village Hall  (map)
  • Cost ~£65  (less if more than 10 participants)
  • Certificate provided

Several spaces left. Financial commitment will only be required when minimum number is reached and instructor and hall to be confirmed.
Since we need minimum number to make this viable, I need to have minimum interest by the middle of September and so far we’re still well short. Please do not delay showing your interest or the course may not go ahead this year!

To sign up, contact me on WA or TG or email firstaid@dhpc.org.uk with your name, email and mobile nr.


Rob Urselmann

sections.title: From the Archive
sections.content: Every year the club awards the Cockroft Cup to our most improved pilot.  But who was Keith Cockroft?  Few members will remember, but Martin Baxter digs out the story.
